behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not double-counted as deletion plus addition
behaviors.justification: Git's rename detection is complex—the tool must correctly parse git's rename output (format and similarity threshold vary by git version/config) and classify it separately from add+delete pairs. Mocking git output is insufficient because: (1) rename markers and thresholds vary across git implementations, (2) the tool might misinterpret edge cases (partial renames, boundary cases), and (3) only real git subprocess output reliably exposes parsing failures. A mock could pass while real git fails.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository, commit an initial file, then rename it with 'git mv' and commit the rename. Run the CLI tool on the repository. Verify the output shows exactly one file in the 'renamed' category with original and new names, and does not appear in 'deleted' or 'added' categories.

behaviors.description: Diffs with binary files do not crash the tool; binary files are counted in the output
behaviors.justification: Git outputs binary file changes in a specific format ('Binary files ... differ') that requires careful parsing. The tool must: recognize the binary marker without crashing, parse file paths correctly, and count the file in the right category. Mocking might not expose failures because: (1) the tool could crash only on specific binary formats (images, executables, archives), (2) the parser might fail on edge cases like binary deletions or large binary diffs, and (3) real git subprocess output is needed to test actual error handling. A mock could falsely pass while the real tool crashes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with initial commit containing a text file and a binary file (e.g., PNG image). In a second commit, modify both files. Run the CLI tool. Verify: (1) the tool completes without crashing, (2) both files are counted in 'modified' category, and (3) both files appear in the report by name.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository with no commits yet produces an empty report, not an error
behaviors.justification: A no-commit repository is an edge case that requires real git subprocess interaction to test properly. The tool must gracefully handle the state where git returns no commit history. While error handling logic could theoretically be mocked, real git's behavior in this state (how 'git log' responds, whether it returns empty or error) is only reliably captured by real git. The tool's parsing and output formatting must work with this edge case—a real no-commit repository is the only way to verify this without relying on potentially inaccurate mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a fresh git repository ('git init') with no commits. Run the CLI tool on this repository. Verify the tool exits successfully (exit code 0), produces output (empty or 'no changes' message), and does not raise an exception or error.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness behavior. Counts must be accurate across all scenarios—different file counts, various change types, complex histories. Mocking git output is insufficient because: (1) a mock cannot cover all possible repository states and change patterns, (2) subtle parsing bugs only manifest with real repositories (unusual filenames, large diffs, mixed change types), and (3) the tool's parsing, deduplication, and categorization logic can only be fully validated against real git output. A comprehensive mock would be unmaintainable and miss real-world edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with a known sequence of commits: e.g., 3 files added, 2 modified, 1 deleted, 1 renamed. Run the CLI tool. Parse the output and verify that reported counts of added, deleted, renamed, and modified files match the actual repository history.

behaviors.description: Output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: The tool's behavior with respect to git's staging state (tracked in the index) requires real git subprocess interaction. If the tool analyzes working directory changes, it must report them consistently regardless of staging status. Mocking git output for both staged and unstaged states might not expose subtle differences in how git reports changes depending on their staging status. Only a real git repository with actual staged and unstaged changes can reliably verify this behavior without relying on mocks that might miss real parsing differences.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with an initial commit containing a file. Make a modification and stage it ('git add'). Make another modification to the same file and leave it unstaged. Run the CLI tool twice with the same analysis scope (e.g., working directory changes). Verify both runs produce identical output. Alternatively, create a more complex scenario with multiple changed files in different staging states and verify consistent reporting.
